label: Bag of Words
definition: A text representation that describes a document by which words it contains and how often, discarding word order entirely, turning text into a fixed-length vector of counts over a vocabulary. Its defining flaw is exactly that lost order, "dog bit man" and "man bit dog" are identical, yet for tasks driven by which words are present it is a fast, strong baseline, the one everything later reacted against.

body.text: The bag of words turns a document into a vector by the simplest move that works: treat a piece of text as an unordered collection, a bag, of its words, recording how many times each vocabulary word appears and throwing away the order in which they occurred. A document becomes a long vector with one entry per word in the vocabulary, most entries zero, a few holding counts, and two documents become comparable as vectors, which lets classical machine learning algorithms operate on text they could otherwise not consume. For decades it was the default representation in natural language processing.

The defining and most criticized property is that it ignores word order. "The dog bit the man" and "the man bit the dog" produce identical representations, even though their meanings differ sharply. This is a real loss of information, but for many tasks it does not matter much: deciding whether an email is spam, or which topic a news article belongs to, often hinges on which words are present rather than their arrangement. The representation is also extremely sparse and high-dimensional, since the vocabulary can run to tens or hundreds of thousands of words while any one document uses only a small subset, which suits efficient sparse linear models.

Raw counts are rarely used directly, because common words like "the" and "is" appear everywhere and carry little signal. The standard refinement is tf-idf, which reweights each count by how rare the word is across the whole corpus, so distinctive words count for more and ubiquitous ones for less. The vocabulary is also usually trimmed by removing very common stop words and sometimes by collapsing inflected forms to a root, and a partial recovery of order is possible by treating short word sequences as units, the n-gram model extension, where pairs or triples of adjacent words become vocabulary entries of their own.

The bag of words matters as the conceptual baseline against which everything later is measured, which is the keeper. Paired with naive Bayes, logistic regression, or a support vector machine, it produced strong, fast, interpretable text classifiers and topic-modeling pipelines that remain competitive for many practical problems. Its central weakness, that it has no notion of similarity between different words and no sense of order, is precisely what the next era set out to fix: word2vec replaced sparse one-hot word identities with dense vectors that place related words near each other, and sequence models and transformers restored word order as first-class information. Understanding the bag of words makes clear what those advances were reacting against.

image.alt: On the left, a Renaissance portrait of a woman. On the right, the same portrait cut into small fragments (an eye, the mouth, the necklace, a patch of hair) scattered loosely inside an open paper bag.
image.caption: The bag-of-words model represents a text by which pieces it contains while discarding their order, like cutting a portrait into fragments and dropping them into a bag. The collection of parts is kept but the arrangement is lost, so 'the dog bit the man' and 'the man bit the dog' look identical. For many tasks the words present matter more than their order, which is why this crude representation works.
